org: London Borough of Richmond upon Thames

candidates.stmt:
As a solicitor with degrees in science, business and law, Mark has devoted his professional career on resolving the legal issues relating to the creation and growth of technology-based businesses. Through his career, Mark has amassed knowledge and skills about the needs in building and growing businesses, as well as the pitfalls to avoid.

Mark is a firm believer of enabling people to be the best they can be, in both business and in life. Enabling people to reach their maximum potential is the key to building a vibrant, prosperous, and caring society for all.

This is a simple truth that is often lost, discounted or even rejected in discussions and debates about to how to build, grow and sustain the prosperity of a nation.

To grow a nation's economy the government's first priority must always be to make its people as prosperous as possible. From prosperity comes investment, not just domestic but also overseas investment. Governments that deny people the opportunity to excel through heavy taxation, programs with vague, ill-defined objectives, or unrealistic “social engineering” schemes do the complete opposite. And always remember, behind every Scheme there is a schemer.

Since the banking crisis of 2008 the rate of growth of the UK has slowed down to almost nothing. The economy has technically grown but only due to an increasing population. This increasing population has skewed the growth trajectory of the country downwards with the average person in this country being poorer than they were in 2008.

Voting for a party that increases taxation in a low-growth country with increasing population is not the answer. The opposite is required. A government that puts money into people’s pockets and not one that takes it away. Reform UK is that party. None of the other are. Vote Reform on 4 July.

Dear Elector,
I encourage you to take part in the local elections on 7 May and to make an informed decision that reflects your values. I have created www.avanessov.com with clear information about me and about the Barnes Ward councillor election, should you wish to learn more.
I was bourn in east sheen in 1962, my family have lived and owned businesses in the area since the 1800s ,i reside in the local area,richmond is close to my heart.

I have lived in this borough for almost thirty years and have a wealth of experience in the areas of environmental sustainability and education. My background includes being a founder member of The Friends of River Crane Environment (FORCE), governor of Richmond upon Thames College
and Chair of Governors of Orleans Park School. I am currently working as Chair of The Friends of Twickenham Green to ensure that this valuable community open space is fully maintained for the benefit of the wider community. Should I be elected I will continue to work hard for the residents
of Fulwell and Hampton Hill particularly to protect historical and much loved assets such as Shaklegate Lane allotments. Should I be elected I will serve you with the enthusiasm and
dedication I have employed in these other roles.

I have lived in the borough for 25 years and married here and brought up two boys here who regard Teddington & Hampton Hill as their home despite moving away for their careers.
I've ran three businesses from the borough. The middle of the three being a broadcast tv equipment hire  company based in Twickenham and employing local people.
My reason for standing here is to use my time away from work productively for the local people and environment.  Iam passionate to make Green Party issues a strong element of helping to run the local council. High on my list is to campaign against water pollution and help restore youth clubs back into the area.

Penny Frost has lived and worked in this borough for nearly 40 years - both of her children were born here and attended local schools. As a special needs teacher she supported many vulnerable young people now bringing up families of their own, and they continue to call on her to help them with housing and school issues. Having been elected in 2014, Penny has been Opposition Spokesperson for Education and Schools since 2015, has spoken a number of times on education issues and met regularly with officers from Achieving for Children.
Penny is a volunteer helper and school governor at Meadlands Primary School and is involved with several local community groups, all of which keep her busy and remind Penny how lucky we are here in Ham with all we have to enjoy and protect.

I'm one of 5 Green councillors in the London Borough of Richmond. In my work as a councillor I'm focusing on pushing the Council to do all it can to help residents insulate their houses and install low-carbon technology like solar panels. As a group, we have successfully pushed our Council to become a registered social landlord so that we can provide affordable housing and insist on the highest standards of energy efficiency of that housing. We've also made sure that our Council gives more help to residents on low incomes and we've highlighted the fact that the LibDem-run Council is pursuing thousands of residents for unpaid Council Tax when they are eligible for reductions. We've successfully encouraged the LibDem administration to make it easier for residents to retrofit their homes and we've also successfully pushed for more community safety officers to help prevent antisocial behaviour. 
Before I became an active member of the Green Party I used to be involved in a lot of community projects like a hydro power project on the River Thames, and running energy advice projects to help people save money on their energy bills and cut carbon emissions. I also ran projects to help residents in Lewisham and Greenwich get grant funding for vital upgrades to their insulation and heating systems and until recently I worked for a renewable energy company that installs solar pv and electric vehicle chargepoints.

Hey there!

My name is Jaydan Okunola, a second year law student at KCL and director of a community interest company (thetcsfoundation.org) dedicated to, amongst other things, working with schools to reshape the education experience for children with trauma and SEN. I have also coded an App dedicated to supporting people with their emotional and mental health needs.

I am keen to bring energy and rigour to the workings of our council;

If elected I pledge to:

- Contest TDRA passionately to preserve our homelands 
- Pressure the Liberal Democrats to commit to more affordable and social housing
- Ensure there is not a monopoly of 'Forest' e-bikes in Richmond
- Protect small business in Richmond by reviewing rent prices
- Work with local schools to reduce the attainment gap and boost political literacy amongst young people
- Ensure an open line of communication and maximum transparency with residents

Your vote is crucial to holding the Liberal Democrats accountable on Richmond Council!

Joe Broughton has lived in the borough for many years. He has a keen interest in supporting local shops and ensuring the new local train franchise, South Western Railway, delivers an improved service for local people. Joe says “As a manager of a team in the technology sector I am committed to ensuring the Council is responsive to local residents’ concerns.”

Lesley Pollesche has lived on the Woodlawn Estate for over 35 years. She is an executive member of the Safer Neighbourhood Board for the Borough. Lesley works closely with the Police and residents to improve our safety in the community. She is also a Governor for Heathfield School and takes a keen interest in education. Alongside John and Michael, she is working with Whitton Community Centre trustees to deliver a new,
modern centre. Lesley enjoys reading and history.

John Coombs lives on Percy Road. He is retired having worked in the media and music industries. He was first elected for Heathfield ward in 2002 and has sat on the Planning and Health Overview and Scrutiny committees. John is the Vice Chair of Planning and has been the arts and culture lead for the last 8 years. He is a Governor of Heathfield Schools' Partnership and is a trustee of a couple of local charities. John has helped hundreds of people with casework and has taken the lead on housing issues in the ward.

Michael Wilson lives locally (in the ward) with his wife and two children. He is a former campaigns manager for a national disability charity and is the primary carer for his two school age children. Michael has helped deliver the borough's popular MegaSkip programme, road safety improvements and the St George's crossing in Whitton High Street. He (along with John and Lesley) are working with the police to tackle antisocial behaviour and they hope to  deliver over the next couple of years a new modern fit for purpose community centre with much needed social housing. Michael loves running and attends St Stephen’s church in Twickenham.

Michael Dingemans has lived in Barnes for nearly 20 years. He has dual Dutch and Irish nationality and worked in publishing abroad before moving to the UK in 1995 and setting up his own publishing business. He and his wife recently opened a small shop locally and he is keen to ensure that the council helps small businesses thrive in the borough to provide a greater diversity of independent retailers. As a father of teenage children, he is also keen to emphasize the importance of providing local opportunities for young people.

Nancy Baldwin is a long-time resident, Nancy has seen many changes in North Richmond. Her toddler group has served hundreds of young children and their parents for over 20 years. Her two adult sons attended Sheen Mount School where they benefited from the excellent education on offer. This is why the lack of state nursery places and the educational funding cuts now facing our local schools are areas of particular concern for Nancy. When not working with toddlers, Nancy works as a career coach and can be found walking through Sheen Wood with her husband on her way to Richmond Park.

Richard Pyne has lived with his family in the North Richmond and Sheen areas for over 25 years. He works as a Management Consultant advising foreign companies on how to set up businesses in the UK. A former Governor of Christs School, Richard cares passionately about proper funding of schools in the Borough. For many years he has served as a horticultural volunteer at Kew Gardens

Richard Warren has lived and worked in Richmond for 20 years. A strong believer in the importance of community, he volunteers at Richmond Foodbank, and has assisted in other local initiatives from time-to-time, including Sunday lunches for the homeless. A journalist, Richard’s work has been used by many media organisations, ranging from the Financial Times to the Big Issue to the BBC. Richard believes local charities, co-operatives and social enterprises have a key role to play in building stronger communities.

**Who I Am & Why I'm Standing**
South Richmond - The Hill, The Green, The River, The Park - is the jewel in the crown of the best borough in London. I've lived here for most of my childhood and adult life, and I could not be more proud to stand as its representative on the Council.
 
I'm not a smooth-talking politician or a committee dweller. My working life has been in business - building companies, solving problems, making things happen. If elected, I will work my hardest to deliver for you. In business I have always seen that 'action has magical power'. I think the same is even more true for local communities. 
 
**What I See**
After 40 years in this place, I see both its extraordinary beauty and some worrying signs of decline. 
- Our high street is hollowing out, serving day-trippers coffee, but failing the community that actually lives here. As visitor numbers grow, litter spreads, services creak, and nature struggles to repair itself. 
- Rising house prices, driven partly by 'Brand Richmond', are pushing out young people, workers, and artists. 
- And beneath the surface, we are failing to provide the services our most vulnerable residents need and deserve: young people with learning difficulties, adults struggling with mental health, older residents feeling isolated and unsupported.

I suspect Richmond has started to believe its own hype. 
 
**In our Council, I see complacency setting in**
The Lib Dems hold eight of every nine seats, and that dominance has snuffed out debate. The emphasis is on the status quo, not imagination — and in a fast-changing world, that means drifting backwards.
 
We cannot rest on our laurels. We must fight to build this great community — the way the generations before us fought for it.

**Why the Greens**
I stand for the Green Party because nationally they are asking all the right questions — and daring to hope for better in a worrying world. Locally, I see their compassion for people and the environment, and their genuine love of t

I work in a charity helping adults with learning disabilities and want to fight for a more humane society that takes care of the vulnerable and marginalised. I believe protecting the local environment is essential to safeguarding our communities. As a party, we are committed to renationalising water companies and providing genuine opposition to Thames Water’s destructive sewage pump. I have a vision for a political system that enacts positive change rather than fuelling resentment and division.

James is an active disability rights campaigner and this is his key focus in this election. Volunteering at Ruils Independent Living in Teddington. He is the Disability Officer for Twickenham Labour Party. He is a member of The Teddington Society and Tedcare. Currently, he is putting pressure on the council to build a Changing Places Toilet for the Borough. James also supports saving the Shacklegate Lane Allotments and believes that it is essential for the wellbeing of the people of Teddington to have these secure. In this upcoming election a vote for James is a vote for supporting the people of Teddington and bringing an effective opposition to the Council.
